channel.title: NYT > Health
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Officials’ Strict Stand on Ebola Leaves Health Experts ‘Stunned’
item.description: Public health experts say the administration’s quarantine orders go beyond what is needed to prevent the U.S. spread of Ebola and hantavirus.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Jacey Fortin
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Jay Bhattacharya, the acting director of the C.D.C., issued quarantine orders for two cruise passengers who wanted to leave the Nebraska facility and isolate in their home states.

item.title: U.S. Adds Security Measures at Dulles to Receive Citizens Who Have Been in Ebola Outbreak Region
item.description: The Department of Homeland Security directed all flights carrying certain travelers to arrive at Dulles International Airport in Virginia.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Aleksey Kondratyev for The New York Times
item.media:description: The government is putting in place “enhanced public health measures” at Dulles International Airport.

item.title: Deadly Measles Outbreak Sickens Thousands of Children in Bangladesh
item.description: The outbreak, with more than 8,000 confirmed cases and another 60,000 suspected infections, has prompted an emergency vaccination drive.
item.dc:creator: Saif Hasnat and John Yoon
item.media:description: The health authorities in Bangladesh have confirmed more than 8,000 cases of measles in the outbreak.

item.title: Experimental Drug Yields Dramatic Weight Loss
item.description: People who got the injection, retatrutide, lost 28 percent of their body weight on average after 80 weeks, Eli Lilly said.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata and Rebecca Robbins

item.title: 2 Minnesota Autism Therapy Providers Charged in $46 Million Medicaid Fraud Case
item.description: The Justice Department claims that clinics used fake diagnoses and kickbacks to parents to bring children into treatment.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff and Ernesto Londoño
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump focused attention on fraud in Minnesota after news reports and a social media video from a conservative content creator last year.

item.title: Ebola Containment Efforts May Have Been Hindered by USAID Shutdown and CDC Cuts
item.description: Aid cuts by the Trump administration have shut down crucial disease surveillance networks and medical supply chains in East Africa.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Stephanie Nolen
item.category: United States Agency for International Development
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Jospin Mwisha/Agence France-Presse via Getty Images
item.media:description: Monitoring visitors in Goma, in the Democratic Republic of Congo, where Ebola cases have been confirmed, on Monday.

item.title: Ebola Crisis Sparks Debate Over Global Health Double Standards
item.description: To some Africans, the claim that the continent’s largest health agency had already bungled its response scratched a familiar wound.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Red Cross workers carry the coffin of a person who died of Ebola at a health center in Rwampara, the Democratic Republic of Congo, on Wednesday.

item.title: A U.S. Reckoning Over Chemical Pollution From Military Bases
item.description: New Mexico is suing the federal government over PFAS contamination from Cannon Air Force Base. The outcome will affect how courts treat more than 15,000 similar claims nationwide.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Nazaryan and Nina Riggio
item.media:description: Art Schaap, right, a dairy farmer in Clovis, N.M., and James Kenney, of the New Mexico Environment Department, stand in the area where Mr. Schaap had to kill his cattle.

item.title: Ebola Was Identified in Congo Weeks Before W.H.O. Declared an Emergency
item.description: Early surveillance and testing failed to identify the rare species of Ebola responsible for the current outbreak. An American doctor is among the confirmed cases.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:description: At a hospital in Ituri Province, Democratic Republic of Congo, the center of a new Ebola outbreak.

item.title: Children’s Mental Health Visits Have Shot Up, Research Shows
item.description: Doctor’s visits for children’s anxiety rose by more than 250 percent over 10 years, according to a study of nearly two million children.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Barry
item.media:credit: Annie Flanagan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Visits were counted as mental health visits if a diagnostic code was included in the claim, either because the child or the family raised the issue or because the child screened positive for mental health symptoms during the visit.

item.title: Abortion Pill Lawsuit Leaves Trump in a Political Bind Ahead of the Midterms
item.description: Louisiana wants the Food and Drug Administration to curtail access to the medication. Doing so could cost Republicans at the polls.
item.dc:creator: Pam Belluck and Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.media:description: A lawsuit against the F.D.A. threatens access to medication abortion, and the Trump administration has remained strikingly silent about the case, even as it reached the Supreme Court.

item.title: TrumpRx Adds Generic Drugs, With Mark Cuban, GoodRx and Amazon
item.description: President Trump announced the addition of 600 medicines to his online drugstore as he appeals to Americans concerned about high drug prices and affordability.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins
item.media:description: President Trump and Mark Cuban during an event at the White House on Monday.

item.title: Insurers’ Delays in Approving Medical Care Persist, Despite Promises
item.description: Doctors and patients complain that the controversial practice of prior authorization for treatment and procedures is still widespread.
item.dc:creator: Reed Abelson
item.category: Blue Cross and Blue Shield Assn
item.media:credit: Lindsay D'Addato for The New York Times
item.media:description: Candace Rond, center, with her daughters Gabby, left, and Sophia, who both have juvenile arthritis.

item.title: Straus Family Creamery Issues Voluntary Ice Cream Recall
item.description: Straus Family Creamery asked customers to throw out some tubs sold in May across 17 states. No injuries have been reported, the company said.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:description: Straus Family Creamery voluntarily recalled some tubs of its organic ice cream, including strawberry pints and quarts, which were on sale in May.

item.title: Caught Flat-Footed, a City Races to Catch Up With Ebola
item.description: The deadly virus has spread alarmingly in Congo for months. Only now is the response taking shape.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh and Arlette Bashizi
item.media:description: Disinfecting the public market on Saturday in Bunia, in the Democratic Republic of Congo’s northeast, as part of the response to the Ebola virus’s resurgence.

item.title: U.S. Ebola Travel Ban Faces Criticism From Congo Health Officials
item.description: Kinshasa residents continue to pack markets, bars and public transportation, despite growing international concern about the spread of the virus.
item.dc:creator: Justin Makangara and John Eligon
item.media:credit: Justin Makangara for The New York Times
item.media:description: A crowded market on Saturday in Kinshasa, the Congolese capital.

item.title: 5 Takeaways From a Times Investigation on Autism Therapy Clinics
item.description: A rapidly growing industry often overprescribes treatment to young children with autism, who spend as many as 40 hours a week at the facilities.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff, Margot Sanger-Katz and Asmaa Elkeurti
item.media:description: A clinic for autistic children in Concord, N.C., that uses applied behavioral analysis, one of the largest and fastest-growing kinds of medical care for state Medicaid programs.

item.title: How Profit-Seeking Autism Clinics Can Harm Kids
item.description: Profit motives are shaping the care inside an expanding autism therapy industry, creating conditions that can harm some children. Our health reporter Sarah Kliff explains her new investigation, co-authored with Margot Sanger-Katz.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff, Margot Sanger-Katz, Erin Schaff, Paul Abowd, Jon Miller and Whitney Shefte

item.title: Short Naps, Long Hours: How Autism Clinics Squeeze Medicaid Dollars Out of Preschoolers
item.description: The industry has grown rapidly, straining state budgets. A focus on finances has led to overbilling, fraud and even harm.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff, Margot Sanger-Katz, Erin Schaff and Asmaa Elkeurti

item.title: U.S. to Block Entry to More Noncitizens Who May Have Been Exposed to Ebola
item.description: The Trump administration announced plans to expand a ban on entering the United States to legal permanent residents who had been in the Democratic Republic of Congo, Uganda or South Sudan.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Hamed Aleaziz
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health care workers check temperatures as people pass through a checkpoint in Goma, Democratic Republic of Congo, on Thursday.

item.title: On the Ground in South Sudan: Why Akobo Faces an Ebola Risk
item.description: Hunger and conflict are on the minds of the residents of Akobo, where an outbreak could have devastating consequences.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg and Ed Ram
item.media:credit: Ed Ram for The New York Times

item.title: Pigeons and People Have Been Frenemies for Longer Than You Think
item.description: Bones discovered at an archaeological site in Cyprus suggest the birds have been strutting around human settlements since at least 1400 B.C.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Nuwer
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: A new study leads scientists to wonder whether people set out to domesticate pigeons or the process occurred naturally as pigeons “colonized” human environments.

item.title: Neanderthal Dentistry, and the Scientist Glad Not to Have Experienced It
item.description: The prehistoric hominins “apparently were very adept at what we would consider invasive medicine,” said the anthropologist John Olsen.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz
